commentBody: I found that to be fun and well clued, and also I had a heckuva time getting the last few letters right.

Fine Friday fare. 
62A and 36A. 
Thanks, gents!

The clue for 19D is wrong. And would be for any puzzles posted between early March and early November. I understand that any qualifiers such as "may be" "can be" or "in summer" would break the misdirect, but that doesn't make it any less wrong. I feel like Bill Clinton arguing what the definition of is is, but Hollywood is on PDT, not PST.

On another note, I have a goal of doing at least one Midi without looking at any of the down clues. Today marks the second or third I've come up one short. Soon enough...

Regarding the column:

I knew about the Mammoth as an NHL team, but I figured their home was SLC.  

I also thought that "coats of white" was going to refer to snow.

Interesting how sometimes a cigar is just a cigar, and sometimes a kitty is just a kitty.

I found the acrosses pretty tough, and didn’t have much on my initial pass. And one of the entries that I “got,” I didn’t actually get.  I had antesup instead of CATSITS.  Luckily the downs were, for the most part, more obvious and helped bring the puzzle together.  I thought that, overall, the cluing was clever.  I especially liked “comment for one who’s going along” for WHENINROME, “create ripple effects, in a way” for SKIPSTONES, and “white collar figure” for PRIEST.

Guess I need to phrase this differently for the emus:

Speaking of SEχMANUALS:

“Want to come to this Indian οrgγ?”
“Sure, do I need to wear anything special?”
“No, just Kama’s you are.”
“OK, Sutra self!”
(Or, to ask another way—“Sari, not sari?”)

Yes! Yes! Yes! Beautiful torture for 38 minutes. Over my average! I’m so happy. I made it through without looking anything up and that most delicious of experiences: “I’m never going to finish this one without help!“ Thank you for the fun before I go to bed.

Sometimes the ones that stump me the most annoy me, and sometimes they impress the heck out of me.

This was definitely the latter.  Almost double my average Friday time, and I enjoyed it enormously.  I just hope Saturday isn't this hard, or I'll be at it all night.
Oof, that was hard.  But very satisfying to complete!

Hope no one HATESON this puzzle, because I thought it was TODIEFOR. I had almost nothing until somewhere near the bottom and even then not sure how I got a foothold, in retrospect. A ton of fantastic misdirects, loved the MEDSCHOOL clue (natch) and STARTERKIT and TAXEXILE not far behind. Even the non misdirecting clues were well written and interesting—ANTICOMEDY (I was thinking of what we used to call “Shaggy Dog Stories”) and WHENINROME. Would expect nothing less of these great constructors. The NW was particularly tricky for me as I had SEDATE before ATEASE (there’s a DOOK) and EGGS for the scallop clue before EYES. Well done, could have even been a Saturday, certainly! If tomorrow if even harder I look forward to it.

I just did not care for this one at all, for some reason.

Half of the fun of misdirected cluing is that you get the “aha” with a few letters left. I had quite the opposite experience: only after slogging through crosses did the answer reveal itself, and by then the “aha” had become “oh, huh?”

HTTP was a great example. Like, sure, it kind of makes sense once you already know it. But the clue is so vague that you could ONLY ever get it on crosses—a brutal position for 1D that puts you on the back foot immediately.

Other toeholds felt similarly torturous and/or nebulous (TAX EXILE? SKI AREA? SEX MANUALS?) Most of these are outside of common parlance, or have components easily interchanged (park for AREA, e.g.)

Most answers just fell into this “lumpy” category for me where I’d maybe have part of a compound word that could go a number of ways, leading to more frustration than fun. But that’s just my two bits.
YOOHOO-- pardon, "I'm this-a way!"-- I just sent you a "Ding thing?", so turn down your EARLAP and read it. After all, as they say, [Comment from one who's going along].

Whew. That was a challenging and chewy puzzle!  Glad I managed to get it completed.  Northwest corner almost got me. Then I got PST, realized that 1D must be HTTPS, and it finally fell into place. 

Very good puzzle!

PSST! That was proper good fun, that was.

Rafael Musa and Geoffrey Schorkopf did not lay any eggs here - my POW for sure.

The grid had me all kinds of twisted, but it was fun looking for a place to land. Just when MUGS were SORTIEd, the antes were not placed, but we'll be havin' well-tended moggies. ATEASE, twas it?

And the icing on the cake is Sean McGowan's column. Like the Stiffy by the Liffey, he gets his point across. Informative, delightful, hi-larious! Pure gas, lad! Another slow pour, please.

Very nice. Big improvement on yesterday. I came in just under my average time and struggled with the NW corner. I, too, couldn't let go of DENT, sharing it's second and fourth letter with the correct entry as it does. I notice that happens in a lot of crosswords, the correct answer sharing key letters with an intuitive but wrong alternative answer, and I always wonder if it's intentional or just dastardly serendipity. If it's intentional, that seems like next level constructing to me...evil genius.

Once again, I stumbled through a late week puzzle, barely, taking cosmic scales of time to do so.

I thought this was tough.  1D, for example...I defy anyone to get that without at least two crosses.  So open-ended.  I'm sure I wouldn't have thought of it had I not worked in computer security for a bit.

I hope this wasn't all that easy for the crew here.  I'm almost afraid to look at the couple dozen posts already made.

Very Friday worthy, methinks?
I plodded along making slow progress, like I should expect for a Friday. 

 “25 minutes. Okay a couple more cells to go”

But that NW corner. 
I couldn’t not decipher 1D. 
I had THERENOW. So I need a word that is _T_P
“I am sure it’s HATESON. but there is no word that starts with ht_”

10 minutes of filling in and erasing the same four cells.  It finally dawns on me.  I loved it. 

Thank you constructor AND EDITORS! for this nice Friday. 

(Now I will go to read the comments, which will all say “too easy!”)

"Ding thing".  Wow! 

I thought I was a genius well I came up with "bEll".

I figured it out. I found the song that was going through my head since we were talking about "counting noses" a couple of days ago. I was called "Tici Ti Tici To Tici Ta", and was recorded by Lou Monte in 1961. https://www.youtube.com/watch?app=desktop&v=wjm5AQWyJfY&ra=m The lyrics I remembered were almost perfectly accurate, and though it wasn't Dean Martin, he's referenced in the song. I knew I wasn't dreaming it up.

When it comes to sleeveless garments, I have a vested interest in them.

(They suit me just fine!)
peTSITS almost undid me.
Gotta admit I've never seen EAR LAP rather than ear flap.

This is a Friday puzzle worthy of its place in the week. I struggled with much of the cluing. (I have doubts about EARLAP but most other tough clues left me slapping my forehead when they resolved.)

I finished eleven minutes slower than my Friday average, but it feels, I don't know, earned?
Mistake at 20A.  The answer should be MOOPS.
But... Hollywood is currently on PDT not PST...

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Sometimes the right amounts and proper densities of vagueness, mist, murk and multi-directionality instead of straight-up Algonquinesque precision result in a proper Friday/Saturday grid.

Like today's.

And based on my archive dives they're becoming much less frequent.

Some really artful examples back in the 00's and 10's.

Thanks, setitors.
Solved a bit faster than my average, I say with just a bit of braggadocio, but the initial fill looked like the mythical handful of spaghetti tossed against the wall!

That was the perfect combination of some solid garbage-free fill and some very clever cluing. Based on the constructors' notes I assume the editors retained a lot of the original cluing in today's puzzle. More such Friday puzzles please!
Crunchy!
“MANIOC”?  Hmm.  Had to look that one up.  CASAVA is what we most often call the tapioca tuber.  Always learning . . .  Thanks.

That was kinda awesome - properly challenging but also enjoyable and very satisfying to solve. There was just so much misdirection! Finally! That happens so rarely at the NYT these days I almost literally feel giddy when it does. Great job  constructors, and cheers to the editors for apparently not messing it up.

It took me the longest time to figure out ELF HAT and TED TALK - both things are very American and not really present in my psyche. Our Polish Christmas lore involves no elfs (or elves), so their attire is largely alien to me. I tried listening to a few TED TALKS but every time I thought there was something irritatingly infantile about them: as an academic I just cringed at all the oversimplifications, and the obviously much rehearsed, hammed-up enthusiasm of the speakers rubbed me the wrong way, too. Too much drama, too little substance (incidentally, for similar reasons I generally avoid American documentaries).

I was familiar with RONDA and KYIV, but for NAST, NHL, KROFT (I wanted KRaFT so badly there once only a few letters were missing! And knowing Lara Croft didn't help) I had to rely on crosses - but I appreciate how the constructors avoided nativity crossings.

I don't rate puzzles but if I did, this one would be 10/10.

Enjoyably difficult Friday!

I was sure I knew KOFI Annan, but for the longest time the cross had me convinced it was mPH / mOFI. Blame it on my fundamental Americanness, but my brain simply doesn’t reach for “KPH” as an abbreviation. After some 10 minutes of hunting for my mistake, I flipped from the imperial to the metric system and heard the music play!
Proper Friday.

Thank you for an excellent crossword puzzle that didn’t require gimmicks or paragraphs of explanation, and instead relied on simply being appropriately challenging. Thursday through Sunday should always be like this.

bald, then soft, before SORESPOT
SKImSTONES before SKIPSTONES  
SARee before SARAN (we call it clingfilm)

Didn't know MANIOC or EARLAP (how is it not flap?!)

Definitely a challenge for me this morning. Good Friday!

I love the crossing of Julius Caesar’s [Beware the ides of March] in ACT I,  the CHERUBs of [Raphael’s “Sistine Madonna”], and the proverb WHEN IN ROME.  

And since I’ll be visiting soon I thought I would repeat the origin of the phrase. It is attributed to Saint Ambrose, a theologian and statesman who served as Bishop of Milan from 374 to 397. As the story goes, Saint Monica and her son, Saint Augustine, discovered that Saturday was observed as a fast day in Rome, where they planned to visit. However, it was not a fast day where they lived in Milan. They consulted Saint Ambrose who said "When I am here (in Milan) I do not fast on Saturday, when in Rome I do fast on Saturday". That reply is said to have brought about the saying "When in Rome, do as the Romans do”. 

I hope to do just that. 

A very enjoyable puzzle. Thanks guys!
Great fun that was

It would have been more appropriate if the answer to 8A was A TEASE rather than AT EASE, because that’s how this wonderfully ticklish puzzle felt to me! 

Keep ‘em coming!

Today’s amusing inventions:

Pointy red and green ELK HAT, of course — those fraternal societies get up to all kinds of antics. And please note, the plural of elk hat is elk hat.

Scrumdiddlyumtious had to be TOTES YUM. Oh wait, guess not, because part of the answer was used in the clue. Still, you all are welcome to use “totes yum” as you see fit.
Too many proper nouns, especially in the southeast corner, but elsewhere also. Who is Ronda Rousey? Cartoonist Nast?

I can see what an excellent crossword this is, even though I couldn't do some of it.

Interesting that quite a few people immediately thought of gambling at the word 'kitty' when I never would, and thought cat. 
A lot of comedy does not have a punch line, but I've not heard of ANTI COMEDY. Maybe 'alternative' comedy. 

TAX EXILE has been quite a common term in UK, some celebrities have avoided tax this way. It has been used as a plot point in mystery stories -  when you had to be out of the country for the entire year to avoid the tax, but sneaked back for a day and were seen, and then blackmailed.

First pass= one answer (I’M LATE). That was an apt indicator for what was a very slow, but very satisfying, solve. You might even say it was TO DIE FOR.

HIP HIP…. nah, never mind.

I have to disagree with the general sentiment here - That this was a good puzzle. We've been so starved of good puzzles lately, we are conflating difficulty with quality. This one felt like a slog, with very little reward.

Now *this* is my kind of Friday puzzle.  So many clues that could go any which way, forcing me to work the crosses.  

Reading the constructor notes gave me the feeling that much of the cluing was left intact.  Let's do that more often.
I originally had NASH instead of NAST, which left me with.... Well, you can see for yourself what the cross would be, I don't want to upset the emus.
Second City is in Old Town! 😢

Loved it! Agree with everyone about the satisfying difficulty level. But ... EARLAP??? (My last one in ... frowning over why there would be one weird rebus in a Friday ... to find out it was, mystifyingly, correct!)
What are you Americans doing with your ears over there??

Yes.

Just…yes.

Take my money.

(Wait, you already do.)

Take *more* of my money.

Not a comfortable experience. EARLAP? Seriously? ANTICOMEDY? Huh?

I dropped in ELFHAT, half expecting it to be wrong. Delighted to see NAST, creator of Puck, himself. Howdy, KOFI. But, otherwise, yeesh.

Aw man. That was good. I especially liked sink hole for Great depression. I did not like the clueing of nasty fall for ice storm. Tense was off and Those are winter events here in Maine. Very pretty but unfortunately can be associated with power outages that last for days. Spring is here!!

Message to HeathieJ:
I think the word you're looking for will be EARLAP. (Har) The last time it appeared, October 17, 2014, I recall similar reaction. 

I got hung up by sticking with bell instead of DING in the NW for too long.  I don't use any sound notifications for my texts, so was slow on the uptake there. 

Nice Friday puzzle, guys.

Hoooboy!  Tougher than most Saturdays.

I got a little Naticked on the MANIOC/SENOR cross.  I haven’t had the opportunity to visit Mexico City (it’s on my list) and the texture of tapioca gives me the ick.

Loved the cluing on ICE STORM, SINK HOLE, and TAX EXILE.

Satisfyingly difficult - easy Fridays are always a bummer.  

8.5/10

Regarding EARLAP...
Does anyone else remember Walter Mattthau saying this to Glenda Jackson in 'House Calls' [1978]---"Up yours, with ear laps".  I thought that was the funniest thing, for some reason, but when I rewatched the movie a few years ago, it wasn't in there.  Maybe I'm remembering the wrong movie.....

A funny thing about KYIV: My mother was born in Ukraine a few years before the Russian Revolution. She came to the US in 1921 at the age of 8 or 9. I often heard her pronounce the city's name as "Keev," and I sometimes would try to correct her by reminding her that the name was "Ki-EV." I didn't know, and she probably didn't either, that this was the Russian version of Kyiv, and her pronunciation of Kyiv was exactly right.
Never once in a long life seen CASSAVA spelled with a single 's.'  Presumably a variant used solely by crossword constructors.

Yet another fantastic puzzle.
32 minutes and a very satisfied fist pump.

I used to love the two CHERUBS with their little butterfly wings, gazing up in wonder and awe at the young infant and his mother in the "Sistine Madonna". Then they started appearing on every other Christmas card for a number of years,...and the painting became my least favorite Raphael Madonna. https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Raphael_-_The_Sistine_Madonna_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg But, fun fact?, there are not just two CHERUBS in the "Sistine Madonna". If you look carefully at the clouds that surround the Madonna and Child, you will see dozens(!) of little (rather eerie!) CHERUBS!

When 1A starts of with a clue that has "____ in modern lingo, I know automatically that this is not going to be a puzzle designed with this geezerette in mind. But by the time I finished, I not only had filled in that answer, but had also heard the term.

I had not, however, heard of  ANTI-COMEDY. I thought for sure that the Mammoth home was NHM, for Natural History Museum, until I realized that it was probably a sports team and threw the L in. 
Somehow, I pulled MANIOC out of some deep, dark recess of my ancient brain, and there were enough older references  (POOH, NAST) to help the flow along. Instantly thought of SORTIE, but only as a military term; not that I have knowledge of anything military, but I probably picked it up from some old novels. But when I put it in tentatively, it helped me finish the puzzle, the last quadrant to fall.

Finally delighted to have a true Friday puzzle that starts out inscrutable but gets the job done at the end!
I don't comment often, but I thought this especially terrible puzzle earned it.
Was it Renaissance art that morphed a cherub from terrifying to cute?  Rafael's cherubs are very far from Biblical cherubim.

Like a lot of people, I spent a lot of 2020 baking bread. I had also bought a gigantic obsolete plasma screen for next to nothing from a company that had used it in the course of converting the movie "Titanic" to its 3D version, then left it in storage for at least a decade...I set it up in my bedroom and used it, of course, to watch baking shows.

Which is why, for the clue "Hollywood is on it," I had "BBC."

The "rat taxi" paragraph in Wordplay made my day, by the way.
Ear correctors should be offered at all karaoke bars .

Suitably dense and chewy for a Friday. I did have to research the tuber to finish the bottom right.

Now craving my grandmother's tapioca pudding. The ready-made options are way too sweet.

These two have crafted one of the funnest (?) puzzles I've done in ages. So many fine multi word answers and clever clues. A perfect Friday head scratcher for sure. Very well done Rafael and Geoffrey – really looking forward to the next one.

Tricky one! I needed some help with a few people's names and a certain root vegetable, but overall very satisfying to complete.
I was massively held back for a long time in the NW by my 100% confidence in "FEARNOT" instead of "THERENOW", until I noticed I had actually put in "FEARENOT" and confidence declined rather sharply. Key to the solve though!
Always fun to have a good dust-up with a Friday puzzle, so thanks Rafael & Geoffrey!

In the category "Be careful what you wish for," I present this puzzle.
More than once I thought the puzzle was going to defeat me.
The only thing "certain" after my first pass was LOOP and KOFI (and I thought, "Thank Zeus for proper nouns!")

Starting to put in probables, I had BELL (for "Ding thing") and TIC (for "Jerk").  Thanks to PST for correcting the first, but it took too long to remember my high school French and SORTIE.  (Thank Mithras, that wasn't a misdirect.)

Loved the "white coat" clue; took too long to see the "duties" misdirect.  Couldn't understand forever(!) how "Scrumdiddlyumptious" could end in FOR.

A tough, tough puzzle...
And I loved it!

Thanks, Rafael Musa and Geoffrey Schorkopf.
And thanks to the Games editors for letting this go through with its tough clues.
[See how filled with gratitude we can be?]
More, please?

Very fun puzzle.

I especially like the crossing of HTTP and TAXEXILE, and I’m even quite happy with MANIOC, as it’s always fun to get an answer from crosses that’s not just completely unknown to you, but is basically impossible to *verify* once you’ve got it (i.e. “manioc” does not look like a real word to me).

Beyond that, there were a handful of other crosses that took me a minute (e.g. TWOS and SWANKY), but looking back, none of them even strike me as particularly stretchy. Great Friday!

I loved this.  There were many things I loved about it, but the best thing was the curiosity that "Address no longer than 18 minutes" engendered in me.  What could that possibly be?  I just HAD to know!  When TED TALK came in, I practically shouted out: "Of course!!!!"  I love when that happens.

But many entertaining clues and answers.  I especially liked WHEN IN ROME, TWOS for the slam-dunks (which I thought was going to be EASY), TAX EXILE and TO DIE FOR.
Was RONDA the only unknown name?  KOFI was a gimme for me.  But I must say there were some unknown non-name answers:  I didn't know MANIOC and I didn't know ANTICOMEDY as clued.  Come to think of it, I don't know ANTICOMEDY in any sense of the word.

A good mixture of swooshiness and resistance.  I found it very entertaining and involving.

I saluted this puzzle last night, noting 62A and 36A. I'm amused to see that since last night the median solve time has moved up well past 36A. Quite a few people seem to be encountering their first "real" NYT XWP Friday. https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-05-08

I loved this puzzle; so many AHAS. "Hollywood is on it," for one. Some kind of weight-loss drug, right? And I'm quite familiar with RONDA Rousey, but not the spelling of her first name. TIL she was an Olympian before moving to MMA, and a medalist to boot. You go, girl!

I was also intrigued by the division of labor between the two constructors, with one setting the grid, and the other writing the clues. I wonder if that is common.
The northwest defeated me. I had PDT for 19A, and TAXEXILE was not coming for me. The KYIV spelling was a hurdle in the SW, but gettable.
In what world is an earflap known as an "earlap" (10D)? Just admit that you stretched this too far.

I expect I am Not Alone when I say, "I hope this satisfies all of those who were "criticizing, in modern lingo" the clues that were too simple, too easy, too obvious....just "too-too!"

I don't want to TOSS STONES, nope; I want to remain SERENE nope SEDATE nope.  
Why not try to put the ELF HAT in at 46D instead of 45D for some extra fun?  And stick to my guns about fLAP at 10D?
AW MAN....  
For the record, I think "Chichi" implies that  the "swank" is overdone--lacking in elegance.  

This certainly took me a good while, until suddenly it all fell into place.  Nice one, Rafael Musa and Geoffrey Schor-head.
"SKI AREA"? hm...
This puzzle was TO DIE FOR! More like this, please!
Tapioca... maniac?
This was a PR for me... my worst Friday time ever haha - I don't mind. It was tough... but Fridays are always tough.
Very well made and challenging, best Friday in a while
A proper Friday that stretched out far longer than my coffee was hot. Thanks for the toughie! Finally wrapped it in Sunday time, whew!

In a bit of synchronicity, I just watched the new Chaka Khan video, and the opening shot is of the front of the TARA Theater.  The rest of the video is set inside a movie theater, but I have no way of confirming that it's the TARA.

53A [Many a Chilango] SENOR

I learned a new word--Chilango--but have never heard it used.

Originally, it meant someone new to Mexico City but now refers to a native of that metropolis, I guess. 

On the other hand, I believe the word Chicano originally referred to someone who has immigrated from Mexico, but now may also refer to Mexican-Americans born here.

Not sure.

One thing that struck me, but sorta "not worth commenting on"--the "fresh coats of white."  MED SCHOOL students wear a short white coat when participating in rounds or shadowing an MD; the "lab coat" is only for MDs.  
Either way, PhysicsDaughter learned early that white coats meant  Trouble, and they triggered panic until she was old enough to understand more.....  So not fun, especially when a doc would complain about her fretful behavior.
Whew. Hard, but fun!

Honestly not fun. 

Too many answers that just seem fake.

Even after solving it just feels like I've done a chore. Might as well have done some dishes...

First Friday in over a month I couldn't get.  I just couldn't get a foothold other than the SE region I had mostly filled.

After giving up and looking at some of the answers I see a lot of the clues were clever but awkwardly phrased enough to explain why I couldn't get them with only one or two crossing letters.

That one felt like a Friday puzzle. Solved it unaided, but took more than 35 minutes---with quite a few fixes needed along the way, like STARTERSET --> STARTERKIT and KIEV --> KYIV. Very glad that both MANIOC and RONDA came to mind today.

Hope to see more Friday puzzles like this one, and an even more challenging puzzle tomorrow!
I thought "manioc" was too obscure to be prominently placed in the grid.  Otherwise, I enjoyed the puzzle.
No gold star today, got caught out by 80% of the grid still being empty and no idea what to put in there. So anyway(s)....

I found this puzzle really difficult today, especially the northwest corner.  That's okay, I like a challenge, and I finished it after taking a break, but man oh man, is it ever bringing down my Friday average!!!
I’m not great at these, but is ‘earlaps’ a regional term? I was stumped because we call them earFLAPS.

There have been complaints in the past about humble brags. So I'll just make a gloating brag brag.

This one appears to have been VERY HARD for most, but it was completely in my wheelhouse. For the first time ever, I beat the median solve time on XWStats. And by a lot, at that. (I'm usually some substantial multiple of that time.)

It helped that I dredged up the French word for "exit" from French 101, figured Raphael would have CHERUBS, always have to NEATEN for company, have lived through many Atlanta ICESTORMS (including one completely amazing one in March of about 1978 or 1979 after all the flowers were out and got encased in what looked like an inch of clear glass), my grandmother frequently called YOOHOO out of her back door, and I have done CATSITting before. 

The only wrong across answer that held me up for awhile was that I had Prebake for 'crispy crust' for awhile.

Unfortunately, none of this makes me feel any smarter. Just feel as though I drew the lucky card today. Actually feeling a little nervous that the rest of my day may compensate.

Hope the chewiness was fun for most! Gotta take a Dutch exit right now.
Tough but fun puzzle! One quibble: is "http" *usually* followed by an s or *often*? It kind of threw me.
Absolutely brutal!
The gold star isn't enough today. The Times should award at least a platinum star for completing a puzzle like this.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ i’m the city who feeds the day with minutes
where the time to go is where you say you look
away
d/ before the year’s gone
before the traces of going along
it’s a date
it’s a wrap
it’s good riddance all
you might go back to the beginning
and a/ skip manuals
Great puzzle, though it would have even better if MANIOC had been crossed with MANIAC.

I haven't done today's puzzle yet, but I would love some advice about the Midi. I finished today's Midi (loved it) and tried to show it to my husband so he could see the graphics. We share an account and he prints it out every morning to do it by hand, so I wait and do it after that. When I tried to show him my completed puzzle on my Mac it first tells me congratulations on completing it but then shows me an empty grid. This is quite frustrating. Can any of you bright sparks out there 'splain it to me???
I was kinda "hating" on the NW corner when, AHA! I got it.

Never commented before and I appreciate your work, gives me great joy. With that said I just came to say I powerfully hated this puzzle. Writers please enjoy your weekend, but this was oddly punishing and I hated it. That’s it.
A lot of obtuse answers with vague clues, not much fun when there is little opportunity to get started anywhere. Earlap is not a thing for example.
Given it’s late spring, shouldn’t Hollywood be on PD(aylight)T?
Whew, this was a tough one! Almost had to give it the old Letterkenny Leave (https://youtu.be/MyjeE7rmO2k), but hung in and finished it.
Wow. One of the best Friday puzzles in a very very very long time. Thanks R and G...so dang good!!! Defintely the cat's meow!!
This was a real workout!

Loved this one... even in its most esoteric moments it still felt fair, and out-of-the-box thinking was rewarded over familiarity with trivia. A great example of how even unorthodox fill (what the hell is an EARLAP? who on Earth calls cassava MANIOC?) can work if more accessible crosses are available as stepping stones. That balance is make-or-break for later week entries and this puzzle nailed it. Fun!
Whew this was a really tough one for me! So many where I had the wrong answer filled in. Once I figured those out, I was able to muddle through the rest fairly quickly. Overall a slow solve.

Now *that's* the NYT Friday crossword we know and love! 

I'm delighted to say I needed look-ups and a "check puzzle" halfway through, not to mention feeling like my brain was being PAN SEAREd. 

Just beautiful! Bravi to Rafa and Geoffrey, and Will Shortz and the editing team.
I don't get to feel suddenly absurdly smart, which is how it should be. 
And these are the puzzles that allow me to feel legitimately proud of the ones I actually do nail without any checks.

My only brag on this one was knowing MANIOC. 
The manioc flour ("farinha de mandioca") is ubiquitous in Brazil and, toasted with butter and onions (with other variations as well), is an essential accompaniment to black bean stew ("feijoada") but often enjoyed with the run-of-the-mill black beans and rice.

One cannot have lived in Brazil and not know tapioca and MANIOC. 
Funnily, I only came to know the tuber as cassava here in the States, so I felt rather avenged for always calling it MANIOC.

I only had two points of honor with this solve.
That was the first one.
The second one is having done did it, as Eddie would say. 

Many thanks to the 
outrageously talented Rafa and Geoffrey!

Miserable, tough puzzle. For example, many, many smart phones vibrate when a text is received - IRRC, there are other options. 

Hence, "the sound of getting a TEXT" is hopelessly vague.

About 45 minutes in, I leaned heavily on 'Wordtips".
This is one of the worse ones in recent memory . . .
Good lord, this was the hardest puzzle I've done in a while.
Chewy.  Good Friday.
13:13. A few minutes quicker than average. Good crunch for a Friday. Liked the call-and-response of the identically clued “PSST” and “YOOHOO”. Someone is blowing someone’s cover.

It was very hard until it wasn't so hard, and then it was hard again.

Chichi and the cap accessory were among the hold outs. Don't remember ever knowing chichi.

Overall, it was a fun puzzle. At 47:48 unaided, I got my monies worth.

I've posted here many times about my displeasure with the puzzles being dumbed down.  I won't be doing that today, as it was obtuse, oblique, and a total joy for what I expect from the NYT.  I know the trend was hatched to attract new puzzlers, but considering you now have 3 different crosswords, why not configure one for new solvers and another for those of us who have challenged ourselves for many years?  Of course, they should be identified by non-insulting terms.

Puzzle find today. A Monday from January 6, 1997 by Sidney L. Robbins. Three 15 letter theme answers in that one, each of them clued as "Disoriented." KNOCKEDFORALOOP DISCOMBOBULATED FRUSTRATEDNOEND Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/6/1997&g=37&d=A ...
It’s mph in the uk not kph
Oof. This one took me quite some time.  Quality Friday, if not frustrating at times.
Earlap is not a word.
The heck is an EARLAP?

YOOHOO! IMLATE, but I still wanted to say what a fun puzzle this was! Terrific collaboration! After a lot of thises before thats and a lot of head scratching, I finished up in exactly my average Friday time, to the second. Wow! Although I do have to say that we don't do French goodbyes or Irish goodbyes here in the Midwest... This here is how the Midwest rolls. https://youtu.be/MHCmE4ABnNs?si=zM24S4ex6RauyvhX

Wow.  Very tough one for yours truly.  After one pass through all the clues there were only a handful filled in.  Amazingly, all but one turned out to be right.  I had to resort to looking up a number of clues and then had to resort to reading the intro line by line.  Got three clues, then the puzzle started to fill in.  As someone else mentioned, there were a bunch of "Aha!" moments and more a couple of dope slaps to the old noggin.  Not a "win" for sure, but it was a good Friday puzzle.  Congrats to all who completed it.

Absolutely despised this puzzle. If humor without a traditional punchline is ANTICOMEDY, I’m guesstimate by a crossword with so much slop is ANTICROSS?

So many vague clues, answers that work but are not real phrases. 

I could go on, but I wasted enough time on this garbage. 

Rating:
1/10

More hard puzzles please!! Loved being stumped on a Friday. I only needed one hint for things to fall into place, and some trust in my crossings. Once unlocked it solved smoothly and cracking it was al lot of fun.
Loved it loved it loved it! Ten thumbs up, WAY up!

I love it when I get through a first pass with almost no gimmes! Fun one, in just over my average (to be over at all is a rarity lately!). Still not sure about “I’m this-a way!” though—since it's not a Mario/Italian stereotype reference per the column, I guess a variation on "he went that a-way"? But can't remember ever running into "this-a way." Oh well!

On finally completing this bruiser (with quite a few lookups), I returned to the top page to be met by "It's time to relax. Pick a puzzle." Those words have never felt more like a horror movie tagline.
Good puzzle save a few that went too far to ignore. Anti comedy was unforgivable. No need to go further. As I said that alone is unforgivable and to comment further would be overkill

I had to finish yesterday's puzzle before doing this one so I'm very late to the party. After reading only a smattering of comments I see many loved it, many hated it, not much in between. I found it a challenge, for sure, but also really admire it, so thank you Rafael and Geoffrey.
I admit to checking out some of Sean's Tricky Clues, which I often do on late week puzzles. I was surprised he didn't help us out with EARLAP, an alternative to "earflap" that was definitely new to me. Pretty tricky.
Be well and safe, fellow puzzlers.

Once I figured an EARLAP was not an EARFLAP like I thought, and figured there must be something called an ANTICOMEDY I was not familiar with, I still had to deal with my mental block that "first gear" could be STARTER KIT rather than STARTER BRA like I first thought. Oh yeah, the "ding thing" I also had misfigured as BELL instead of TEXT. Took longer than usual then but I eventually came around to it. Good themeless.

That was such fun - and it went SO much better once I ditched "antes up" as the answer for "feeding the kitty" 😸

Thank you both!

oh the NW corner, you vexed me so!

i did enjoy the rest of the friday puzzle

happy friday everyone!

I found this hard, with some word-checks and finally revealing the last square, X, for TEXT for a Ding thing. Haha! Also didn’t think of TAX EXILE and had pET SIT after ANTES UP for a while. Loved ACT I for source of “Et Tu, Brute?” because I’d been trying to think of a name. A lot of good ones: like ICE STORM for “Nasty fall” is I guess my favorite.
Really liked this one. Had some great cluing and not too many proper nouns
Not to be dramatic, but someone should be in jail for the clueing at the crossing of TAXEXILE and TEXT.
Great solve! Though Hollywood isn’t technically on it right now.
On today's publication date, Hollywood is on PDT, not PST.
It fun. If you have a list of five or six answers that don’t work for nearly every clue, then something is amiss.

@Dan I don’t think I’m selling myself short when I say that I could do these things for another hundred years and I could never get a puzzle like this in 14 minutes or however, long it took you. It doesn’t make me feel bad, though. I loved it so much. This is what we wish for.

@Jake G

I’m telling you, Jake: just do a whole lotta Wednesday-Saturday puzzles in the archive, and I’m guessing you’ll get there fast enough (I previously mentioned Fridays and Saturdays, but as long as you go back at least ~10 years, there are actually quite a few tough Wednesdays, and so many fun Thursdays).

It’s kind of like jogging with a weighted vest, or how baseball players often warm up with weighted bats when they’re on deck — once you get used to struggling with the old ones, 99% of the new ones feel like a walk in the park.

Incidentally, today’s puzzle reminded me of many Wednesdays from yore: overall, the clueing was easy-medium, but with a handful of entries that make it tough to get across the finish line (e.g. MANIOC…and a good chunk of the upper-left corner).

Jake G, I would have said the same thing about myself in the 1990s when after 30 years of solving crosswords I began to time myself daily ... and make sure to look up any answers I hadn't solved or understood.

@Steven M.
I thought the exact same thing, while solving.  I saw "PST" and refused to enter it for a long time for the reason you cite.

@Steven M.

Eh, I disagree. It’s true that Hollywood is on PDT at the time of this puzzle’s publication, but given the lack of any qualifiers (as you mentioned), I view PST as an evergreen answer — whereas if the answer was PDT, I would definitely want some kind of nod in the direction of “sometimes” or “right now”.

By convention, crossword answers need not be true all the time, or true for all cases, or all examples of the clue, etc., etc.

PST and PDT are both valid responses to the 19A clue. How many AHAS does it take to know which one to use?

userLocation: There is such a thing as too much couth.
commentBody:
@Steven M.
Am I the only one who thought of the Pacific Coast Highway PCH or variants thereof? I was forced into PST by crosses but didn't get it until I got here.
@Anonymous PT is the evergreen answer. Standard and Daylight Time are two specific variations of the time zone, which is called Pacific Time. Standard specifically means not DST.

@SBK in TO
Hollywood is several miles east of the PCH. (Good to know if your GPS breaks.)

@Anonymous While it's true that your source does indicate a concept of standardized time being referred to as standard time, nothing I've seen in your source or elsewhere uses Pacific Standard Time or PST as a stand in got standardized Pacific Time. In fact, multiple sources make the clear distinction that I do, only considering PST an acceptable usage for non DST months https://www.time.gov/ https://www.timeanddate.com/time/zones/pst In addition, your source mentions early distinctions for what is now known as DST, such as EPT or EWT instead of EDT.
@Anonymous Which is why clueing Hollywood as PST in summer hours is inaccurate in a 2026 crossword, even if it would have been accurate in 1883

@Steve L

And sometimes a French exit is just a French exit, and not an Irish goodbye.
@Marshall Walthew you and I will not be the only two that had ANTESUP at first. In a sense, we probably were supposed to. 🤪

@Whoa Nellie

A pox on spellcheck, which has no wit.

@Jake G
Yep. I did

@Jake G

Took me almost twice as long, but otherwise the exact same for me.

@Jake G Lots of comments complaining about the difficulty and taking over their average time to solve. Those people should stick to the Mondays. I've been doing the Fridays (and a lot of Saturdays) too fast lately. Which is a bummer because I look forward to the challenge. I don't care about my average, I'm a glutton for this kind of frustration. I don't want to know everything, I want to figure it out. Today's was a decent challenge, but I still have my hair.

@SP

I had serenE instead of ATEASE.

Got the right interpretation of white collar but slow to realize that neither Parson nor Pastor were going to absolve my solve.

Liked the up to date Mammoth clue and 
the shoutout to Molly Goldberg.

@SP
I had placid before ATEASE and felt pretty good about that. Awesome puzzle, like days of yore, ERE things got too easy.

@Al  in Pittsburgh
Y'mean like "Yoohoo, Mrs. Bloom?"

userLocation: There is such a thing as too much couth.
commentBody:
@SP
 ATEASE and EYES were both in the NE, no?

@SBK in TO

Oops that’s what I meant

@Edward

So for those of us looking back to more challenging days, those are the kinds of nebulous clues we long for.  But diff’rent strokes, I suppose.

@Edward

Your three examples of torturous and/or nebulous answers seem like normal phrases to me.
@Edward Modern youth has its limitations. Tax exiles, ski areas, and sex manuals were all common parlance when I was coming of age.

@Edward

I really know what you mean.  I understand the slippery, unsettled feeling that one gets deep into the puzzle, with a lot of scary white space and even more iffy answers.

But, like SP and Steve L says, this is what a tough puzzle has--lots and lots of answers that could go one of two or three ways.  I was stuck with TheEXILE for a long time.  Who'da thought the answer would have not one, but two Xs in it.

Likewise, I had tAkESON for a long time as well.  

This was a doozie, IMHO.

@Edward

Is only been 1:40 since puzzle drop. But I’m surprised this is the top comment. 

I guess the entries could be considered “green paint”. But they are legitimate things. 
Though I’ve never heard of tax exile.  
Tax haven. Yes. 
Tax exile, I assume means leaving the country and never coming back. 

But I guess the point for me is that the cluing was actually fun. I mean wordplay fun. 

And gave the puzzle a level of difficulty that I think many solvers here long for. But of course, YMMV. and that’s cool. It’s great that you were so thoughtful in stating your dissatisfaction.

@Edward

This seems to be more a complaint about difficulty than quality. Of course most answers in a NYT Friday should be solvable only once you have a few crosses… that’s the point of a CROSSword. It would be a rather dull affair if you could simply deduce every single answer from the clue alone… the vagueness is a feature. HTTP is a pretty odd example too: once you have HT, what else is it going to be? 

The same is true of the rest of your examples too… just because something is nebulous doesn’t make it bad… again, this should be a feature of late-week puzzles. Fairly harsh assessment, given the cluing is largely excellent, and there are plenty of AHA moments. 

I think this really just reflects that people have become too used to the simplistic cluing that has begun to characterise late week NYT crosswords the past 5 or so years, which is a shame.
@Edwardi felt exactly the same. And I'd argue that http isn't really _usually_ followed by an "S"? Sometimes, sure. Even often. But it depends on the site.

@Edward
Like SP says, different strokes. I thought the puzzle was great. For me, the "indecipherable" clues, like HTTP and PST, were interspersed with gimmes, like KOFI, ACTI, and ERE. I fell into several traps, like antes up before CATSITS.

I thought HTTP was brilliant. Isabeau says she got it with no crosses! And I was curious, these days almost every site is HTTPS. I have seen a trend towards that in the past several years, and a poke around browsing history showed me that every site I'd been to recently was HTTPS. The answer came slowly, partly because I've found that I'm almost always ignorant of "modern lingo", so I give it a wide berth.

I've never heard ski park. It's a ski area or maybe resort.

@Edward

The Rolling Stones became TAX EXILES from the UK to France in early 70s to escape 90%+/- taxes and remain solvent. There, they recorded EXILE on Main St.

@Edward HTTP had an incredibly vague clue, sure, but gettable with any 2 crossing letters since it's such a weird combo. It is Friday after all. I thought TAX EXILE was a great clue, and SEX MANUALS is surely not that obscure. I'd agree with SKI AREA, a very bland and generic description I don't think I've even seen before. Maybe it's an actual thing in areas that have skiing...

@Edward

I agree that many of the clues were vague and difficult to decipher,
But, for me, that's part of the fun of Friday and Saturday puzzles.  They're supposed to be puzzling.

A note on "toehold": I use that term for when I get a word that I'm not sure of (usually a short one), and it makes me see other answers that were previously obscure, opening up an area of the puzzle.
It seems that one of us is using the term incorrectly.  (Or have I misunderstood you?  Or maybe the word has multiple meanings?)

What do others think?

@Edward
It depresses me that this is the top comment.

@Matt

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Francis

And yet I'm sure you've wracked up a solve or two after being positively convinced you're "definitively stuck."

For me these are the most eye-opening puzzles.

Plus, as you are a man of science, I feel obliged to point out to you that being a perpetual motion machine in any sense would violate the first and second laws of thermodynamics.

@Matt

😂  A friend of mine used to say he made a perpetual motion machine, but it ran out of gas.

Yes, you are right that the puzzles solves longest delayed have the most intense effect...and yet I really need some fail-safe.  Not so much anymore, but when I was in school I was famous for spending all night on one problem, screaming and punching walls and cursing the cosmos...

I can let go easier these days, though.

@Francis

I hated biology classes in school. They were all about rote learning. We had these textbooks crammed with facts, written using language I did not understand. I had to learn it all by heart. The section on mitosis and meiosis was especially tough. It was nearly 30 years ago but to this day I remember the term "endoplazmatyczne retikulum" - whatever the Φ it means. Well, anyway. I was so angry at having to memorize all that bee was that as I was sitting there over my book, I kept hitting it with a stick and cursing. [Slam] Φ [Slam] Φ [Slam] Φ [Slam] Φ (...). I ended up punching a hole through a dozen pages.

Fun times.

Not.

But yeah, I occasionally solve impossible puzzles.
"bee was" was supposed to be "bee ess", duh.
Jesus... *Over* 30 years ago. My head is a mess.

@Andrzej

I remember feeling myself under a lot of pressure.  

There was a commercial on TV when I was probably six or eight, in which there are a bunch of older kids standing outside this elegant fenced gate into a campus.  A guy comes up to the fence, unlocks it, and only one or two go in.

That commercial chilled me to the bone.  And when I couldn't solve something, that commercial would often come to mind.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

The first two years of medical school for me were precisely as you describe, though correspondingly more maddening and intense, I suppose (med school v. high school).  Endless hours, days, weeks and months of mindless memorization which I liken to learning the names and locations of every item on every shelf in the grand supermarket of human corporeal existence.

No room for thinking.

Almost did me in for the insult and mindnumbingness of it all.

Only in the third year did there appear light, thought, room for subtlety and the poetry of human experience.

@Matt

This was primary school, mate. We had to learn hundreds of polonized Latin pages by heart as tweens... 

In Law School I had to memorize loads of stuff but I didn't mind it - it was my choice to be there, and I knew I had to live with it. Understanding the underlying logic of the legal system helped, too.

But school before that... Jesus. One of the reasons I went into law was that it had not been taught to me before. Biology, math  physics, chemistry - all I remembered and still remember from those classes was trauma...

@Matt
The educational point of having to memorize all of the stuff on the front end is that, at some point when you have enough memorized, you start to see the connections and understand the process. If you only take one course in biology, that synthesis never arrives, and it is just memorizing a bunch of things you won’t remember in a few years.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Mark Smith

Therein lies the rub:  doctors are not pedagogs and never study how to teach.  "Memorizing all that stuff on the front end" with no clinical context is a foolproof technique for ensuring it will be mostly and quickly forgotten.

The landscape is changing; clinical content is being used increasingly to contextualize the supermarket rote.

But not nearly quickly enough.

@Hitch Agreed. Finally a Friday that took (me, at least) more than 15 minutes. Some people really care about their average times. It's just you against the puzzle. A solve is a solve and easy is no fun. I want to get to the point of brute-forcing answers to try and get something to take shape. Today's didn't quite get me there, but still gave me a good test.

"So, what *are* your favorite Raphael Madonnas, X?" I hear you asking. I'm glad you asked. Number 1: The Alba Madonna https://www.nga.gov/artworks/26-alba-madonna Number 2: The Madonna of the Meadow https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madonna_del_Prato_(Raphael) Number 3: Madonna of the Goldfinch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madonna_del_Cardellino And if you want to know why Raphael is the greatest painter of this theme, I'll be happy to tell you. Or, better yet, go to the Metropolitan Museum in NYC and go look for yourself!

commentBody: @Alex One of my favourite Nash-ian rhymes sets "cherubim and seraphim" against "bailiffim and sheriffim". See here: https://ogden-nash-poetry.blogspot.com/2009/03/kindly-unhitch-that-star-buddy.html

@The X-Phile
More than the EYES in a scallopPP
I left out "La Belle Jardinière"! Certainly deserving of being in the top three! https://smarthistory.org/la-belle-jardiniere/

@Mean Old Lady

I suspect that, in both instances, it would be difficult and unpleasant to count.

@Times Rita 

Watching coverage of the first Gulf War drilled SORTIE into my permanent vocabulary.
@Times Rita where’s the POOH? I’m assuming this is a typo, but can’t figure out what for.

@STS
I worked on another puzzle right after that, so I may have put it here mistakenly.

@Kevin
I think all those who yearn for harder puzzles deserve their 'bone'.  Of course I found this Friday horribly hard.  I would have given up with the first clue.  But that's where the 'Easy Mode' clues come in.  (The link to them comes in an email you can sign up for.)  I needed twenty one of these lifesavers.   Christina Iverson's efforts shouldn't go unused!
@Kevin This oughta rile up the troops

@Kevin

I don’t know about riling up the troops but these sorts of comments do irk me. Not because you didn’t like it, which is fine; nor if you thought it too hard, if that’s the case; not even if you thought it unfairly hard if you give some examples to back it up (or even if you don’t). But to just say “this is a terrible puzzle” is just very self centered and annoying. It obviously pleased a lot of people, including myself, so on that basis alone it isn’t a terrible puzzle. It’s just a puzzle that YOU found terrible, or detested, or found unfair, etc. I am very respectful to people who make respectful negative comments, but I disrespect comments that seem to make global statements about their own experience, especially in an unnecessarily hurtful way.
@Kevin - I don't comment as often as I used to, but I thought this especially terrible comment earned a reply.

@SP
How exactly is his comment 'unnecessarily hurtful'? He had an opinion, and wanted to share it. The EXACT same way you do. Doesn't matter that it 'pleased a lot of people'...he's not taking a poll. Bottom line: You disagree with his POV, that's all there is to it...so call it what it is. You both are complaining online...at least he's honest about it.

@Ben Blackwell

I don't think you're seeing the point.

The problem isn't expressing your opinion; everyone's entitled to do that, and everyone can, of course, like or dislike whatever they choose.
The problem is expressing your opinion without giving any reasons for your opinion.  Without some rationale, there's no possibility of a discussion.

@Ben Blackwell

You're partly right.

Comments that say "Great puzzle!" and nothing else bore me, but I don't reply to them, even if I disagree.  They're being supportive of the constructor at least.

Comments that say "Awful puzzle!" and nothing else also bore me, but when I disagree, I want to hear the complainer's rationale.  Why do they have a different opinion than me?  What did they think was wrong with the puzzle?  If they want the constructors to make better puzzles, they have to offer constructive criticism.

@The X-Phile

As a birder whose art conservator daughter taught to appreciate Renaissance art, it’s the Madonna of the Goldfinch for me.

userLocation: There is such a thing as too much couth.

commentBody:
@Anonymous
What is notable about the HTTP/TAXEXILE cross?

@Niall

Oh, I just thought those were both fun entries, and they were a couple of the last ones I got (largely because I was also slow to get TEXT, which I found less fun).

With HTTP, I guess the fun part is the under-specification of the clue (so many things can be usually followed by an l
“s”), which makes it seem almost unreasonably open-ended…until you’ve got a couple crosses.

And although I wasn’t previously familiar with the term “tax exile”, it instantly made sense, and elicited a chuckle out of me, as soon as I was down to the leading “T”.

@Anonymous
Ah, fair. I thought I was missing something obvious about them.

@Nancy
MANIOC is supposed to be poisonous unless cooked properly; back in "Radio Days" we listened to a mystery where someone had poisoned a victim in just that way....so I tucked that bit of info away just in case I needed it at some future time.  And here we are, just 69 years later....Voilá
@Mean Old Lady -- And you may never need it again after today -- unless, of course, you're planning to poison someone.

@Nancy
Moi??

@Grant Great question! Anecdotally, I know co-constructors who have divided the labor this way before, so it's definitely not unheard of. Just like solving a crossword, there are many different ways to build one. Glad you enjoyed the puzzle!

@Grant - Common or not, that division of labor worked well for this team. This was a fun puzzle (though I'm not great at sports trivia).

Took me forever to get the upper left corner because I thought 3D [Ding thing?] was either 'bell' or 'gong'.

@Rick

Um, in this world? Just because you don’t use the term, or have heard of it, doesn’t mean it doesn’t exist and just looking it up in a dictionary should confirm that for you. Agree it’s less common, but that was t your point.

@Rick Yeah I'm with you on this one. Nobody says "EARLAP", that was like literally 1000 years ago. 12th century stuff. It's just confusing when you think the crossword genuinely has a missed letter in a word.

@Mean Old Lady

I had SEDATE and TOSSSTONES first too

@Tim
I’m not sure I understand your question mark. I may be in France now, but I lived 30 years in WY and CO. I’ve only called them ski areas or ski resorts. And I sure wouldn’t call Eldora a ski resort. Nor A Basin. Those are ski areas. Or the mountain, but only in a lot of context.

@PhilO
You’re off by one letter

@Vaer

TARA, of course, for those who don't know, is from the very popular classic American novel, "Gone With the Wind." It was Scarlett O'Hara's family's ancestral home.

@lucky13
Well yes, TARA appears here frequently. Atlanta and "fictional estate" in the clue made it fairly obvious...and Twelve Oaks did not fit.

@Xword Junkie
STARTERSET for me too, thinking of the set of socket wrenches Dad got me for Christmas. I have a rolling tool chest now.
Where in the grid would you have preferred it be placed?
(This was not a dig at the crossword. It was just too hard for me)
@MamaJRA I found earlaps is the name for a cap with ear flaps. Never heard of it before either!!
@Sue S thank you!

@Lynn
We were living in Connecticut during the ICE STORM of 1973. I was pretty young, but I remember that Dad had to drive to New Jersey to buy a chainsaw.

@Lynn

I, too, had PREBAKE at first.  
When I got the first A, I switched to PARBAKE, and thought myself very clever.
When I got the N, I wanted to put in PAN FRIED, but it didn't fit.
It took (seemingly) forever to get PAN SEAR.  It was that kind of puzzle today.
Ain't it grand?

@Bob Adler
The S stands for secure, referring to the website you are visiting. Surprise, some sites are not secure.

@Bob Adler - My browsers are set to alert me when I attempt to reach a site without the "S". 

I get an alert maybe 2-3 times per month, tops.

All the other sites I visit are HTTPS. In other words, the vast majority. But I don't spend time on the dark web or at porn sites, so there's that. 

YMMV.
Deliciously so!

@Anonymous
yep, I did try multiple times. It really doesn't matter I guess but it is slightly annoying. Thanks for your response though :-)

It's getting late in the day, so more people will probably recommend a post saying "Earlap is not a thing for example" in shared frustration than will read much less recommend a post pointing out that it *has* been a thing since before the 12th century. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/earlap
Crossword convention: answers need not be true in all cases or at all times. PST or PDT are equally responsive to the clue. Only one of them gets the AHAS in this puzzle.
@derek, I'm familiar with manioc from Brazilian cuisine.
@sotto voce, I know manioc from Brazil too; my husband lived there for 8 years!

@Susan E

Oh, that's so cool! And I don't know if you know this, but one of the constructors, Rafael Musa, is originally from Brazil, which made me think that maybe that's how MANIOC got snuck into the grid...
So, does your husband like "farofa" or does he equate it to sawdust as some of my friends do? Lol. 
I was young when I started on it, so I adore it and always add it when I make black beans.

BEWILDERED
